title: The Disappearing Index Effect
abstract: The abnormal return associated with a stock being added to the S&P 500 has fallen from an average of 7.4% in the 1990s to less than 1% over the past decade. This has occurred despite a significant increase in the share of stock market assets linked to the index. A similar pattern has occurred for index deletions, with large negative abnormal returns during the 1990s but an average return of only 0.1% between 2010 and 2020. We investigate the drivers of this phenomenon and discuss implications for market efficiency. We document a similar decline in the index effect among other families of indices.

article.introduction:
One of the early andpersuasive challenges to the efficient markets hypothesis was the observation that stock prices react to investor demand unrelated to fundamentals. In particular, Shleifer (1986) and Harris and Gurel (1986) showed that stocks added to the S&P 500 index experienced abnormal returns of approximately 3% around the announcement of an index change. Since these studies, an extensive literature has documented similar price impact in other stock indices such as the Russell and the MSCI, as well as many other settings in which investors buy or sell for reasons unrelated to fundamentals, including mutual fund inflows, mechanical reinvestment of dividends, price pressure around mergers, and Treasury auctions.1
The initial studies of S&P 500 changes were performed during a time when index investing was nascent. Shleifer (1986) shows that S&P 500 announcement returns were smaller prior to 1976 than in the 1976 to 1983 period he focuses on, consistent with more dollars tracking the index leading to more price pressure. Driven by flows into passive mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs), index tracking has continued to grow at a rapid pace over the past 40 years. We estimate that funds tracking the S&P 500 in the form of mutual funds or ETFs have grown from essentially zero in the 1980s to approximately 7% of market capitalization in recent years. Other estimates based on trading volume (Chinco and Sammon (2024)) or sell-side research suggest even higher levels of investor indexation to the S&P 500 today.
Changing composition of additions and deletions: One possible explanation for the disappearing index effect is that the nature of firms added or dropped from the S&P 500 has changed over our sample. We quickly rule out the possibility that the effects we document are driven entirely by changes in the characteristics of additions and deletions since the 1980s, although such shifts do account for part of the changes. For example, the size of additions and deletions relative to the total capitalization of the S&P 500 has been shrinking over time. This could help explain the disappearance of index inclusion and deletion returns because, empirically, the size of an added or dropped firm is strongly related to the magnitude of the index effect. Using a simple regression-based approach, we show that changes in composition (as measured by Wurgler and Zhuravskaya's (2002) “arbitrage risk,” trading volume, size relative to total index capitalization, and coverage by sell-side analysts) account for only a small portion of changes in the average index addition and deletion returns that we observe in our sample.
Migrations: Another possible explanation is that thenetdemand shockDexperienced by the typical index addition or deletion is smaller than it appears. We show that in recent years, an increasing percentage of index additions and deletions are “migrations” from the S&P MidCap index. When these stocks are added to the S&P 500 index, they simultaneously leave the S&P MidCap. In these cases, forced buying by S&P 500-tracking funds is simultaneously matched with forced selling from S&P MidCap-tracking funds, leading to a smaller net demand shock. From the 1990s to the present day, migrations have increased from about 50% of additions to over 70%. The trend toward more migrations is even stronger among S&P 500 index deletions. The differences in returns for migrations reflect the increasing importance of the S&P MidCap index over time. In the mid-1990s, migration and nonmigration additions had average returns of 10.2% and 6.7%, respectively. By the 2010s, however, direct additions had returns of 5.4%, while migrations had returns of –1.8%. This divergence coincides with the rise of MidCap-focused funds.
Front running: A third explanation is that index additions and deletions have become more predictable over time, attracting arbitrageurs who front-run index demand. Under this explanation, sophisticated market participants who anticipate index changes purchase additions and sell deletions before the announcement day, leading the price to move before the official announcement. In the extreme case in which index changes can be perfectly anticipated, we would expect no abnormal returns at all during the period between announcement and when the index change occurs. While we do not have position-level data to measure front-running activity, we can measure preevent returns and find mixed evidence to support this hypothesis. In recent years, returns in the three months leading up to the announcement have become somewhat stronger, although the reason for this is subject to interpretation. In addition, a simple rule of selecting the largest eligible firm has become a better indicator of future S&P 500 addition, further suggestive evidence of which lends further support for increased predictability. That said, predicting exactly which stocks get added remains difficult, and we find very little change in the returns experienced in the 20 trading days before the announcement, during which time one would expect to see higher returns if there were substantial anticipation of index changes. Overall, our assessment is that this explanation has played only a limited role in the decline of the index effect.
Increased liquidity: The last explanation we consider is that the stock market has simply become more efficient in terms of providing liquidity to S&P 500 index additions and deletions, that is, in the context of equation (1),Mhas declined. We show that even after accounting for the increase in migrations changing our estimate of the average demand shockD,Mhas indeed declined by a factor of approximately 20 for index additions and to an even greater extent for index deletions. When we evaluate this hypothesis together with all the other explanations we consider, we reach the same conclusion.
Why has the market for index changes become so much more elastic? Part of the answer is that the stock market is more liquid today than in the past, in the sense that trading costs in other settings have also declined since the 1980s and 1990s. However, this is only part of the story, as trading costs have not fallen by enough between the 1990s and the late 2010s to explain the decline in the index effect. Moreover, the decline in market-wide trading costs predates the disappearance of the index effect.
Several market changes are likely to have contributed to the greater provision of liquidity around index events. First, over the past 15 years, Wall Street trading desks have increased personnel and computing resources devoted to index trading, with several large players (UBS, Goldman Sachs) developing specialized sell-side teams. Large passive investors also employ large teams to study and improve liquidity around rebalancing. Second, the distribution of trading volume has become more concentrated around index change events, facilitating liquidity provision (Chinco and Sammon (2024)). Third, despite the large size of the demand shock experienced by additions and deletions, much of it appears to be accommodated by other institutions. Specifically, although index trackers now buy about 7% to 8% upon index addition, total institutional ownership moves little around index changes. We interpret this result as indicating that professional active investors provide liquidity to passive buyers and sellers.
Overall, the findings suggest an account along the following lines. In the 1980s, index changes were unanticipated, index funds were small, and there was mispricing in the market. As index funds grew, the mispricing deepened and turned into an opportunity. As a result, the market adjusted to take advantage of this opportunity, in part by better anticipating inclusions, but mostly by creating arrangements whereby other institutions stood ready to sell to indexers upon inclusions. This worked to eliminate the anomaly on average, despite demand shocks that continued to grow in magnitude over the 2000s and 2010s. In this sense, the decline of the index effect is much like the evidence for other anomalies: they decline once they are well recognized by the market (McLean and Pontiff (2016)).
Our results raise the obvious question of whether the index effect has declined in settings other than the S&P 500, such as the Russell 1000 and other well-tracked indices. Many other papers, including Madhavan (2003) and Petajisto (2011), find evidence of index addition effects outside of the S&P 500. Accordingly, we apply our methodology to several other families of indices, specifically, the Russell 1000 and 2000, as well as the Nasdaq 100 and other S&P mid- and small-cap indices. Overall, we find similar results for these indices in the sense that addition and deletion returns have declined over the past decade. However, the details and magnitudes vary by index, and the statistical and economic significance is generally weaker than the decline that we have described for the S&P 500. For the Russell index additions, the change is not statistically significant. We leave a more complete analysis of these indices to future work.
A long and robust literature documents downward-sloping curves and price pressure for individual stocks. Beginning with Harris and Gurel (1986), Shleifer (1986), and Lynch and Mendenhall (1997), dozens of studies analyze the implications of index changes for stock returns.2Cai (2007) distinguishes between fundamental news and price pressure. Most closely related to our paper is Bennett, Stulz, and Wang (2020), who first noted a decline in the index inclusion effect among additions between 1997 and 2017, and whose work we build upon. Preston and Soe (2021) also document the decline in the index inclusion effect among additions and deletions beginning in 1995. Finally, Vijh and Wang (2022) document lower absolute returns associated with migrations from the S&P MidCap to the S&P 500.
A more recent literature studies the effects of rising passive ownership, including Qin and Singal (2015), Garleanu and Pedersen (2018), Koijen and Yogo (2019), Buss and Sundaresan (2020), Kacperczyk, Sundaresan, and Wang (2020), Lee (2021), Bond and Garcia (2022), Ernst (2022), Coles, Heath, and Ringgenberg (2022), Gabaix and Koijen (2022), and Malikov (2023), study implications for inelastic demand curves for stock prices and the aggregate market. Several papers focus specifically on estimating a price elasticity, that is, the extent to which prices can be expected to move for a demand shock of a given size (e.g., Scholes (1972), Loderer, Cooney, and van Drunen (1991), Bagwell (1992), Kandel, Sarig, and Wohl (1999) Kaul, Mehrotra, and Morck (2000), Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002), Petajisto (2011), and Chang, Hong, and Liskovich (2015)), which we do as well, although no consensus has emerged in the literature to date.3
The paper proceeds as follows. In SectionI, we lay out the puzzle, documenting both the increase in mechanical demand driven by index changes and the puzzling disappearance, on average, of an effect on returns. SectionIIconsiders each of the potential explanations in turn. SectionIIIdocuments the decline of addition and deletion effects in other families of indices. Finally, SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Index Tracking and the Index Inclusio:
In this section, we present the main facts. We first describe how we assemble a list of additions and deletions, before turning to how we identify funds that track the S&P 500. We then present statistics on announcement, effective date, and total returns associated with index changes. In our last set of tests in this section, we examine whether there is any correlation between the net purchases by mutual funds and ETFs tracking the S&P 500 and the returns we observe.
The S&P 500 index is designed to track the performance of large-market-capitalization stocks in the United States and to be representative of the U.S. economy. To be added, the firm must file 10-K reports and be based primarily in the United States. Furthermore, as of 2023, the firm must have a market capitalization of $12.7B or greater, a float-adjusted market capitalization that is at least 50% of this threshold, and an investible weight factor of at least 0.1, that is, 10% of the shares outstanding must count toward the float. Finally, the firm must meet criteria for minimum float-adjusted liquidity, the sum of the most recent four consecutive quarters of GAAP earnings must be positive, and Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) earnings must be positive in the most recent quarter.4At any given point in time, several stocks may meet these inclusion criteria, but a committee inside S&P decides which stocks to add to the index. According to S&P documentation, “Stocks are added to make the index representative of the U.S. economy and is not related to firm fundamentals (S&P (2017)).”
We obtain data on S&P 500 additions and deletions between 1980 and 2020 from Siblis Research. For each index change, Siblis provides the date the change was announced (announcement date) as well as the date the change was implemented (effective date). If an index change occurs on a weekend or trading holiday, we mark the next trading day in CRSP as the announcement or effective date. We merge these events to CRSP on date and ticker, and hand match on names in cases in which (i) there are multiple CRSP permnos associated with the given ticker or (ii) there are no CRSP permnos associated with the ticker. Using this methodology, we match 732 of the 736 additions and 726 of the 731 deletions between Siblis and CRSP over the 1990 to 2020 period. Before 1990, Siblis does not provide information on announcement dates, so for pre-1990 additions and deletions we use data from Barberis, Shleifer, and Wurgler (2005).
For purposes of measuring returns, the full sample that we describe above is sufficient. However, to construct a consistent sample on which to perform all of our analyses, we remove observations that cannot be matched to the Thompson S12 mutual fund holding data on CUSIP, either the quarter before or the quarter after the index change. We also exclude cases in which the firm was listed, acquired, or delisted for reasons other than an acquisition or was an acquirer (i.e., had an ACPERM in CRSP) within 100 days of the index change.5These filters exclude, for example, spin-offs, whereby a security can be added to the index and then quickly removed. Internet Appendix TableIAIdescribes in more detail the original sample as well as the final sample size after applying all of our filters.6Internet Appendix TableIAIIverifies that our inferences about absolute addition and deletion returns declining are not sensitive to sample selection criteria.
To quantify the amount of money tracking the S&P 500 index, we leverage Thompson S12 data on the quarterly holdings of mutual funds and ETFs. Our goal is to identify funds that tend to buy additions, or sell deletions, around the time of an S&P 500 index change. To this end, for each fund we count the number of times an added stock is not held by the fund the quarter before the addition but is held by that fund the quarter after addition. Similarly, we count the number of times a deleted stock is held by the fund the quarter before the addition but is not held by that fund the quarter after the addition. We divide the sum of these counts by the total number of additions and deletions each year to compute the fraction of index-tracking trades made by each fund. We classify funds as S&P 500 trackers if, on average across all years they are present, they perform at least 50% of index-tracking trades each year. According to this methodology, the largest S&P 500 trackers in 2020 were the Vanguard 500 Investor Shares (VFINX), the Vanguard Admiral Shares (VFIAX), the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), the Fidelity 500 Index Fund (FXAIX), and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).7To mitigate concerns of overreaching with our classification of S&P 500 funds, in Internet Appendix FigureIA1we show that we obtain a slightly larger estimate for the size of the S&P 500 tracking industry when we identify funds based on their objective codes and names instead of changes in holdings.
Having identified the S&P 500 tracking funds, we measure net buying and selling by these funds around index changes by adding up the shares held by all trackers the quarter before and after the index change. We then define net trading by trackers asNetTradingi,t=100×(ΔSharesheldi,(t+1,t−1))/Sharesoutstandingi,t+1$Net\ Tradin{g_{i,t}} = 100\ \times \ ( {{{\Delta}}Shares\ hel{d_{i,( {t + 1,t - 1} )}}} )/Shares\ outstandin{g_{i,t + 1}}$, where both shares held and change in shares outstanding are split-adjusted using the CRSP cumulative factor to adjust shares outstanding.
Figure1shows the average net trading by trackers across additions and deletions each year. Consistent with the aggregate increase of passive ownership, in the early 1990s net buying by trackers of additions was close to 0% of shares outstanding, while now it is over 6%. This pattern is mirrored for deletions, going from approximately 0% to almost 8% of shares outstanding.
We believe our estimate of net trading by index trackers is a lower bound for several reasons. First, our estimates are based only on S12 data, that is, mutual funds and ETFs. There are surely institutions of other types, such as pension funds and endowments, with assets that directly replicate the index that are not included in our calculations. Indeed, as Chinco and Sammon (2024) argue, the direct replication industry (i.e., investors who internally replicate indices rather than buy index funds) may be larger than the assets under management (AUM) of explicitly passive funds. Another reason our estimates may be too small is that there are shadow indexers that closely track the S&P 500, but not often enough to be classified as index trackers under our method (Mauboussin, Callahan, and Majd (2017)). Sell-side research estimates the size of S&P 500 index tracking industry in 2022 to be approximately 13%, or about 50% higher than our estimate.8

For announcement returns,Ris the cumulative return between the trading day before the announcement and the trading day after the announcement. For effective date returns,Ris the cumulative return between the day before the implementation of the index change and the trading day after the change. The average period between the announcement and the effective date is 4.8 days for additions and 5.8 days for deletions. Our main interest isCARit$CA{R_{it}}$, defined as the cumulative abnormal return from the last trading day before the announcement to the first trading day after implementation. In principle,CARit$CA{R_{it}}$captures the price impact resulting from the market absorbing net demand from index traders. For most of our sample, index changes are preannounced with much of the return (to the extent that there is a return) occurring on announcement. In the early part of our sample, however, index changes are not preannounced.
Figure2plots the average index inclusion and deletion effect in 10-year rolling windows, while TableIreports year-over-year averages. For additions, the index inclusion effect was 3.4% in the early 1980s and increased to 7.4% by the 1990s, before declining to 5.2% in the 2000s and to a statistically insignificant 1.0% in the 2010s. Our results mirror those in Bennett, Stulz, and Wang (2020), who show a decline in the index addition effect between 1997 and 2017. We find that the deletion effect has followed a similar trend toward zero, albeit in a less smooth way. In the 1980s, firms removed from the S&P 500 had abnormal returns of –4.6%, while in the 1990s they had returns of –16.1%. The deletion effect then fell in magnitude to –12.4% in the 2000s and disappeared in the 2010s, with an average abnormal return of –0.6%.
One data point that stands out in Figure2is the increase in the inclusion effect in 2020, which drove the uptick in the overall inclusion effect in the late 2010s and 2020. This is due to Tesla being added to the index in November 2020, which, as a fraction of the S&P 500's total market capitalization, was the largest addition of all time (Arnott, Kalesnik, and Wu (2021)). Excluding Tesla, the average inclusion effect in 2020 was –3 basis points (bps). In SectionII.A, we examine whether firm characteristics, for example, a firm's size, relative to the total index capitalization, can explain cross-sectional and time-series variation in the index inclusion effect.
TableIalso breaks the total index inclusion and deletion effect into the announcement return and the implementation return.9The second column shows that, for additions, the announcement return has been declining over time. In the 1980s, it was 3.4%, after which it rose to 4.9% in the 1990s and then fell to 4.1% by the 2000s and to 1.0% by the late 2010s. The third column shows that this pattern is mirrored for effective day returns. Specifically, the effective date return was around 3.4% in the 1980s, increased to 3.6% in the 1990s, and then declined to 1.4% in the 2000s and close to zero by the 2010s. The second to last row reports the difference in average returns between the 2000 to 2009 and 2010 to 2020 periods. Across the total, announcement, and implementation returns, this difference is highly statistically significant.
Columns (5) to (8) of TableIreplicate columns (1) to (4), but for firms dropped from the S&P 500. Like the results for additions, the implementation and announcement returns became indistinguishable from zero by the 2010s. Also like the additions, this difference is strongly statistically significant.
At this point, the pattern is clear: returns to index changes grew in the 1990s in a manner consistent with the growing importance of index funds, but then declined slightly in the 2000s before disappearing, on average, in the 2010s, despite a growing index fund industry. A potentially more direct way to illustrate the puzzle is to compare, event-by-event, the return to the size of assets tracking the index. We show this in Figure3, which plots the index inclusion return against net purchases by index trackers, defined as the total change in split-adjusted shares held by index trackers between the quarter before and the quarter after the index change, divided by split-adjusted shares outstanding (multiplied by 100). We find no apparent relationship between net purchases and the index inclusion effect, for either additions or deletions. Specifically, for additions, a regression of inclusion returns on net purchases has a negative slope and anR2of 2%, while for deletions the same regression has a negative slope and anR2of 6%. Thus, not only has the index effect disappeared, but there does not appear to be a stable relationship between net purchases and returns, as hypothesized by equation (1).
article.II. Explanations:
In this section, we explore four potential explanations for the declining index effect in the face of increased index tracking.
We have shown that the average returns of S&P 500 additions have been declining over time. The first potential explanation we consider is that this trend has been driven by a change in the composition of added and deleted firms, rather than a decline in the index inclusion effect itself. For example, larger firms typically experience larger inclusion returns, a phenomenon perhaps driven by benchmarked investors being more likely to buy additions that form a large share of the index, as doing so helps them avoid tracking error (this would not apply to index trackers or ETFs, which aim to track the index perfectly). For example, in 2020 the addition of Tesla to the S&P 500 index—the largest firm ever added to the S&P 500 index, relative to the S&P 500's total market capitalization (over 2%)—drove a positive overall average addition effect, with a cumulative abnormal announcement return of 5.2% and an implementation return of 4.5%.
Another potential change in composition is related to the notion of “arbitrage risk” associated with added and deleted stocks, as discussed by Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002). The authors show that stocks with closer substitutes, and thus lower arbitrage risk, should experience lower price pressure. This is closely related to the idea that price impact should be correlated with fundamental volatility (Kyle (1985), Chacko, Jurek, and Stafford (2008)). An example of changes in arbitrage risk over time is the addition of high-risk Internet stocks in the late 1990s.
A third potential change in composition has to do with the amount of investor attention associated with index changes. In the past, index inclusions may have been a large shock to institutional investor attention, leading to increases in sell-side analyst coverage. In more recent years, however, with greater average information production, the effect of index changes on attention may be relatively less important.
In (3),CARit$CA{R_{it}}$is the cumulative abnormal return from the day before the announcement to the day after implementation;Turni,t−1$Tur{n_{i,t - 1}}$is the abnormal turnover in stockiover the month before the index changes (i.e., turnover (volume/shares outstanding) minus the value-weighted average turnover across all ordinary common shares (share codes 10 and 11) traded on major exchanges (exchange codes 1, 2, and 3) in CRSP over the same month);Sizei,t−1$Siz{e_{i,t - 1}}$is the firm's market capitalization on the last day before the announcement of the index change divided by the total market capitalization of the S&P 500 on the same day;WZi,t−1$W{Z_{i,t - 1}}$is the Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002) measure of arbitrage risk (i.e., the variance of capital asset pricing model (CAPM) regression residuals over the year before the announcement of index addition or deletion);Coveri,t−1$Cove{r_{i,t - 1}}$is the number of analysts covering the stock in the last earnings announcement before the index change based on data from IBES; and1era=k${1_{era = k}}$are dummy variables for 10-year periods (e.g., 1980 to 1989). We demeanTurni,t−1,Sizei,t−1,WZi,t−1,$Tur{n_{i,t - 1}},\ Siz{e_{i,t - 1}},\ W{Z_{i,t - 1,}}$andCoveri,t−1$Cove{r_{i,t - 1}}$. Note that because we include separate dummies for each decade, there is no constant term in the regression.
We start by running the regression in equation (3) without controls for past turnover, size, arbitrage risk, and analyst coverage. This recovers average returns for each of the decades. Results are shown for additions and deletions in TableII, columns (1) and (3), respectively. Note that the averages differ slightly from those shown in TableIbecause we lose a handful of observations where we do not have information on all the characteristics. In the last three rows, we compare the coefficients between the various decades.
Column (2) of TableIIshows the full regression results, including controls. The first quantities of interest are theγk${\gamma _k}$, that is, the residual average index inclusion effect not explained by the past intensity of trading volume or relative firm size. For the 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s, these coefficients are positive and statistically significant. The coefficient for the 1990s is essentially unchanged at 7.4 when including all of the firm-level characteristics, suggesting that the index inclusion effect in the 1990s cannot be explained by these firm-level characteristics alone. Furthermore, consistent with our previous results, the coefficients shrink from the 1990s to the 2000s and again from the 2000s to the 2010s. Comparing the difference between, for example, the 1990s and 2010s, we can see that the decline in the index effect is slightly smaller (in absolute terms) once we condition on the changing characteristics (6.6% vs. 5.6%). The difference, however, is still economically large and statistically significant, suggesting that changing characteristics cannot explain our results.
We next turn to the role of the firm characteristics themselves. The logic of including turnover in the regression is that more liquid firms (i.e., firms with more past trading volume) would potentially have relatively smaller index inclusion effects because the demand shock upon inclusion is a smaller fraction of average past volume. The first row shows that the effect of past turnover for additions has the expected sign, that is, the estimated coefficient is negative, but it is statistically insignificant. The second row shows that the size of the firm being added to the index matters, and the magnitude is economically large. Specifically, being 1% larger relative to total index capitalization would imply an 18% larger inclusion effect. Admittedly, however, an addition of this size is rare as the average addition is 9 bps of total index capitalization while the average deletion is 3 bps of total index capitalization. Furthermore, additions shrank in relative size between 1980 and 2019, going from an average of 12 to 7 bps of total index capitalization, but our regression estimates imply this would only explain roughly 50 bps of the decline in the index inclusion effect.
The third characteristic we consider is arbitrage risk,WZ, which is related to stock-level volatility. Consistent with theory and Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002), we find thatWZattracts a positive and significant coefficient. Finally, we examine the role of analyst coverage and find that it is negative and insignificant, suggesting that prior sell-side attention is not an important driver of index addition effects.
Column (4) of TableIIreplicates column (2) for S&P 500 index deletions. Theγk${\gamma _k}$coefficients are negative each decade and statistically significant only for the 1990s and 2000s. The difference between the 1990s and 2010s is virtually identical when we control for firm characteristics (14.6% vs. 14.6%), suggesting that the changing composition of deletions cannot explain the decline in the index removal effect. Turning to firm characteristics, turnover surprisingly attracts a negative coefficient, which is not the expected sign, but it is not statistically significant. In contrast, size is marginally significant and positive. A possible explanation for this result is that large firm deletions are likely to migrate to the S&P MidCap index, which we discuss in the next subsection. Finally, we find that arbitrage risk and analyst coverage have the expected signs but are insignificant.
The key takeaway from TableIIis that the decline in index effects is not explained by a simple shift in the composition or characteristics of the firms being added or deleted from the index. We note that while not the focus of our paper, composition effects are important for a handful of years, such as the anomalous large average return in 2020 driven by the Tesla addition.
A second potential explanation we consider is that we have mismeasured thenetdemandDin equation (1), and that properly measured demand for both additions and deletions has fallen. A notable type of index change for which this holds is the so-called index “migrations.” An index change is a migration when the stock moves from the S&P MidCap index to the S&P 500 or vice versa. An example of this would be Targa Resources, which was dropped from the MidCap and added to the S&P 500 on October 6, 2022. This differs from additions, whereby a firm is added directly to the S&P 500 from outside the MidCap or SmallCap universe. An example of an addition is PG&E, which was added directly to the S&P 500 on October 3, 2022.
When a stock migrates from the S&P MidCap to the S&P 500, MidCap-tracking funds sell and 500-tracking funds buy. Over the last 30 years, the passive ownership of midcap stocks (i.e., the fraction of these stocks’ shares outstanding) has grown dramatically. It is natural to expect the growth of midcap funds to reduce the price impact of a firm being added to the S&P 500. In particular, we hypothesize that migrations should have smaller index inclusion effects than direct additions, and that the difference between migrations and nonmigrations should be increasing over time.10
To quantify differences between migrations and direct additions, we start by obtaining data on S&P MidCap index changes from Siblis Research. Unlike our data on S&P 500 index changes, which start in 1980, the MidCap changes data set starts in 1995. We follow a similar procedure as that described in SectionI.Ato match these observations to CRSP. We define a migration as any stock that is added to (dropped from) the S&P 500 and dropped from (added to) the S&P MidCap within five calendar days. Figure4shows that migrations have become a steady share of additions and especially deletions. For example, in the 1990s migrations accounted for about 69% of additions and less than 10% of deletions, while in recent years they both make up over 70% of index changes.
To fully understand the impact of migrations, we need to estimate capital linked to the S&P MidCap index. This is more challenging than identifying funds that tracking the S&P 500 because the largest S&P MidCap funds infrequently report their holdings for much of the sample period. Instead, each year we identify S&P MidCap 400 index funds based on names and correlations. To identify funds based on names, we require that the fund name contain variants of “S&P,” “SPDR,” or “S and P” as well as variants of “400” or “MidCap.” To identify funds based on correlations, we first restrict attention to the universe of midcap-focused equity funds (those with CRSP objective code “EDCM” or a Lipper objective code that starts with “MC”) that do not include variants of “Vanguard” or “Russell” in the name. Of these funds, we classify them as S&P MidCap 400 trackers if their returns have a correlation of at least 99.5% with the index itself for three consecutive years. According to this methodology, the largest S&P MidCap trackers in 2020 were the iShares Core S&P MidCap ETF (IJH), the SPDR S&P MidCap 400 ETF (MDY), and the iShares S&P MidCap 400 Growth and Value ETFs (IJK and IJJ).
Figure5shows the sum of these funds’ AUM scaled by the total market capitalization of the S&P MidCap 400 index. As can be seen, dollars tracking the MidCap have grown substantially over time, reaching about 6% of capitalization, slightly more than that of the S&P 500, as we show earlier. This finding suggests that in recent years, stocks that were added to the S&P 500 from the MidCap experienced significantly smaller net buying pressure than firms added directly to the S&P 500 from outside the S&P 1500 universe.
TableIIIcompares the average returns of migrations and direct additions, by decade, for additions and deletions. We find that in each decade the returns to migration additions are statistically significantly lower than those to direct additions, and that this gap has been growing over time. Specifically, for direct additions, the index inclusion effect was 10.2% in the late 1990s, 8.8% in the 2000s, and 5.4% in the 2010s. The large positive return in the last period is driven by Tesla, which, as previously discussed, had a massive return of 53.35% between its announcement and effective dates.11For migrations, the index inclusion effect was 6.7% in the late 1990s and 2.7% in the 2000s. By the 2010s, this effect became negative, at –1.8%. These results are consistent with those in Vijh and Wang (2022), who document smaller absolute returns for migrations between the S&P 500 and the S&P MidCap indices.
Based on the results for migration additions, we would expect that returns to deletions to the MidCap have become less negative over time. This is true, but the effect is smaller in magnitude than that for additions. For direct deletions, the index removal effect was –12.8% in the late 1990s, –15.7% in the 2000s, and –6.9% in the 2010s. For deletions that are also migrations, the index removal effect was –0.17% in the late 1990s, –4.1% in the 2000s, and 0.06% in the 2010s.
Figure6depicts these trends. The top left panel shows that the index inclusion effect for direct additions to the S&P 500 experienced a decline over the past 25 years. The top right panel shows that, consistent with the increased size of the offsetting demand shock due to the rise of MidCap funds, the index inclusion effect for migrations has observed an even larger decline. The bottom left panel shows the returns to direct deletions steadily declined in magnitude, while the results in the bottom right panel are noisier for migration deletions.
To summarize, migrations are helpful for understanding part of the decline in the returns associated with index changes, especially for additions. We return to migrations in SectionII.D, where we account for the effect of migrations in assessing the impact for price elasticity.
As the amount of money tracking various indices has grown, so has the industry of investors trying to take advantage of the trades they make. For example, widespread press reports indicate that Wall Street teams earn large sums betting on index additions and deletions across a variety of indices.12This behavior is not restricted to proprietary trading desks. In fact, many investment banks (e.g., UBS) publish shortlists of stocks they think will be added to various indices for their wealth-management clients.
If index additions have become more predictable, we should see certain patterns emerge in pre- and postaddition returns. Suppose, to start, that index changes were completely unpredictable. In this case, prices should rise around announcement, followed by lower returns over very long horizons. Alternatively, if additions become more predictable, we should see an increase in price before announcement, followed by reversion in the long run. It is hard to test this scenario, however, because of the endogeneity of index additions. In particular, nonindex stocks that increase in value are more likely to be added to the index in the first place.
We start by looking at cumulative abnormal preaddition returns. To do so, we calculate the cumulative abnormal returns starting 100 trading days before the announcement of the index change to 14 trading days after the announcement. Figure7shows that over the past 30 years, the total price change over this period has been steady (albeit in the 1980s the total price change was lower). However, the price spike on the announcement of the index change has become less sharp over time. Specifically, the cumulative abnormal return up to the day before the announcement was 9.6% in the 1990s, 14.2% in the 2000s, and 18.7% in the 2010s. The cumulative abnormal return up to 10 days after the announcement was 17.2% in the 1990s, 17.4% in the 2000s, and 19.8% in the 2010s.
As we note above, one issue with Figure7is that the variables of interest are defined ex post, that is, we are examining firms thatended upgetting added. It could be the case, however, that firms that observed a large price increase in the preannouncement period have become more likely to be added to the S&P 500 over time. In short, while the evidence is consistent with higher predictability, it is not conclusive, because one could equally interpret this evidence as indicating that the S&P 500 has become more skilled at adding the best-performing stocks, as we show below.
TableIVsummarizes the preannouncement returns shown in Figure7. For each decade, we show average abnormal returns for the window beginningnkdays before announcement and ending one day before the announcement, forn= 10, 20, 50, and 100. For purposes of discussion, we focus on the shorter windows because they are less confounded by selection, and because front-running activity seems more likely in the immediate window before the event. As can be seen, additions had average [–20, –1] abnormal returns of 1.54% in the 1990s compared to 2.35% in the 2010s, and deletions had average [–20, –1] abnormal returns of –5.56% in the 1990s compared to –3.03% in the 2010s. Thus, in the immediate window preceding the event, there is no evidence of changes in front running for additions while the evidence runs the other direction for deletions. At longer windows, however, the picture is murkier. Additions had average [–100, –1] abnormal returns of 9.6% in the 1990s compared to 18.7% today.
Another way to test whether additions have become more predictable is to examine whether we can predict which stocks are added to the index. Here, we focus on the most salient characteristics of index additions, namely, they are large stocks that are not in the index, and develop a simple model of S&P's index inclusion rule. Specifically, each month we compute the market capitalization rank of all ordinary common shares traded on major exchanges outside the index.
Figure8plots the average rank of firms that end up getting added, as well as the 25thand 75thpercentiles of these ranks.13While this is an imperfect ranking system,14Figure8shows that, over time, the S&P has picked larger firms on average, with a smaller interquartile size range. However, this result does not necessarily mean that it is easy to predict which particular stocks will be added at any given time, as the average rank of firms added in the last 10 years is around 50. Furthermore, as can be seen, the interquartile range can be quite large, spanning about 40 ranks, suggesting significant randomness in which firms end up getting added.15
To summarize, the evidence on front-running is mixed. Index changes are more forecastable over the last decade than they were in the past, but we do not observe meaningful changes in returns in the short windows leading up to announcement, which casts doubt on index change predictability alone being responsible for the patterns that we see. We return to predictability in the next section, when we consider it together with the other explanations.
A fourth potential explanation is that the market has become more efficient at accommodating the required changes in ownership associated with index additions and deletions, that is, in the context of equation (1), the multiplierMon demand shocks has declined.
Our goal is to estimate how muchMhas declined. This exercise is possible beginning in 1995, when we first have information on index migrations. The top six rows of TableVreport the results, separately for index additions and deletions. For example, in the 2010 to 2020 period, the average abnormal return for 153 additions was 0.80%. Of the additions, 63% were migrations with an average net demand shock of 0.3% of market cap, while the remaining 37% experienced a net average demand shock of 5.6%. This yields an estimate ofMof 0.37, or equivalently, a demand elasticity of –2.72. Repeating this exercise for each decade in our sample, the multiplierMhas fallen by a factor of roughly 20, from 6.75 in the late 1990s to 0.37 over the last decade. A similar pattern obtains for index deletions, withMfalling from 10.76 in the late 1990s to 0.7 over the last decade. In summary, even after accounting for the impact of index migrations, liquidity has increased substantially.
As we note above, our estimate of the multiplier can be converted into an elasticity by taking−1/M$ - 1/M$. We can benchmark this value against the large range of different price elasticity estimates from the literature on index changes. Wurgler and Zhuravskaya (2002) provide these estimates, which range from –1 (Shleifer (1986)) to –37.2. (Kandel, Sarig, and Wohl (1999)). In comparison, our estimate is –0.15 to –0.09 in the 1990s, –0.28 to –0.17 in the 2000s, and –2.72 to –1.44 in the 2010s.
One reason our calculations of price elasticity are smaller in magnitude than previous estimates in the literature (i.e., for a given demand shock, we find higher price impact) is that our estimates for the demand shock are smaller in magnitude. For example, Petajisto (2011) assumes a 10% demand shock, which is larger than the average demand shock that we find at the end of our sample.16
In TableVI, our primary estimates of the multiplierMare based on the event return, computed throughout the paper as the abnormal return beginning a day before the announcement of an index change and ending the day after implementation. However, this calculation omits changes in the index effect that might be driven by higher predictability. With this in mind, the bottom six rows of TableVIalso provide estimates ofMbased on a longer horizon return, computed as the cumulative abnormal return of each addition/deletion, beginning 20 days before the announcement and ending one day after implementation. Here, the results are similar albeit slightly smaller, withMfalling by a factor of roughly seven for additions and deletions between the 1990s and 2010s. In other words, even if we allow increased predictability of index changes to play a role, we cannot escape the conclusion that the multiplier has changed.
These results are presented in TableVI. We follow a structure parallel to that in TableVand present estimates based on (i) the cumulative abnormal return from the day before the announcement to the day after implementation, and (ii) the cumulative abnormal return from 20 days before the announcement to the day after implementation. The estimates in column (1) mirror those in TableV, with a decline in the multiplier from 6.8 to 0.3 (the differences are due to a slightly smaller sample that we can match to the characteristic variables). Column (2) shows that adding controls for characteristics leads to a smaller decrease in the multiplier, but the decline is still significant, going from 6.6 to 0.7.
In column (3), we replace the left-hand-side variable with a longer-window return that starts 20 days before the announcement, rather than a day before the announcement. Here, the level of the multiplier is higher, but it also decreased significantly from 9.1 to 1.3 between the 1990s and the 2010s. In column (4), we again add all of the characteristics and find that the multiplierMstill decreases significantly, from 8.7 to 1.7. Columns (5) to (8) mirror the analysis from columns (1) to (4), but focus on deletions instead of additions. We again find that the decline in the multiplier is robust to both controlling for characteristics and including a larger preevent return, to account for increased predictability.
Our analysis in SectionsII.D.1andII.D.2suggests that, even after controlling for a variety of potential drivers of the index effect (characteristics, migrations, and predictability), the market has become significantly more efficient in accommodating index changes. What led to this improvement in efficiency? While this exercise is more speculative, below we explore some of the potential factors associated with this change, including (i) increases in market liquidity overall and reductions in trading costs in other settings, (ii) coordination of trading around index changes, (iii) accommodation of index changes by active managers, and (iv) accommodation by firms issuing stock. We find evidence for each of these factors but the last.
The U.S. stock market has become more liquid overall since the 1980s and 1990s, in the sense that it has gotten cheaper to trade without moving the price. We consider two ways to measure trading costs. Amihud and Mendelson (1986) suggest the bid-ask spread as a simple measure of trading costs. We use the Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS) intraday indicators suite to obtain measures of the bid-ask spread based on high-frequency data. This data set uses the method in Holden and Jacobsen (2014) to compute the percent effective spread. The percent effective spread is the percent distance from the midpoint of the (value-weighted) average trade each day. Given that our study spans 1980 to 2020, we need to leverage both the second-based version of Trade and Quote (TAQ) data, which run from 1993 to 2014, and the millisecond-based version of TAQ, which run from 2003 to the present. We do not have a good measure of trading costs before 1993.
The red line in Figure9plots the value-weighted average effective spread for all ordinary common shares traded on major exchanges, computed using TAQ data. Value-weighted average effective spreads have experienced a large time-series decline, from 60 to 6 bps. The decline is similar when examining an equal-weighted average of the smallest 100 stocks by market capitalization in the S&P 500. Note that the timing of the increase in overall market liquidity (which is most pronounced in the 1995 to 1998 and 2002 to 2005 periods) does not closely match the pattern of changes we observe in the index effect. In other words, while the changes in the index effect occur over a period of enhanced overall liquidity, this is not the whole story.
The bid-ask spread captures costs associated with small trades near the midpoint. However, the types of trades executed on days of index changes likely do not fit this description: the fraction of shares that need to be purchased by index funds are enormous, currently making up over 7% of total shares outstanding. Accordingly, we also examine the implementation shortfall, that is, the difference between the arrival price and the execution price for a trade which we collect from Virtu Financial. The implementation shortfall, plotted as a blue line in Figure9, fell significantly less than the average bid-ask spread over our sample period.
Over the past 15 years, Wall Street trading desks have increased personnel and computing resources devoted to index trading. Large players, such as UBS and Goldman Sachs, now have specialized sell-side teams. Passive fund managers also employ large teams to study and improve liquidity around rebalancing, according to discussions with several large index investors, including Blackrock, which alone employs a team of over 60 people in index research.
Trading around index changes has also become increasingly concentrated (Chinco and Sammon (2024)). We interpret this using the logic of Admati and Pfleiderer (1988): to coordinate on this “sunspot,” index providers have moved to a system of disclosing which stocks they are going to trade and when (Li (2021)).
Liquidity around index changes has increased, but who provides it? Below we show that liquidity is provided, on average, by other institutions exiting their positions, rather than by other parties such as retail investors. In other words, while a large, dedicated group of mutual funds and ETFs must buy, on average total institutional ownership changes very little around these events. To quantify this, we obtain data on institutional ownership from Thompson 13F and we examine changes in 13F ownership from the quarter before to the quarter after the index change. These changes are tabulated in TableVII, which compares changes in ownership by S&P 500 trackers to the total change in institutional ownership. Despite the increase in the change in tracker ownership, there has been (if anything) a decline in the change in 13F ownership. For example, the table shows that for the average addition in 2020, index trackers buy 7.53% of shares outstanding. However, institutional ownership increases by only 0.65% of shares outstanding. This result suggests that other institutions have stepped up to meet the buying and selling demand from S&P 500 funds. The same pattern appears for deletions: in 2020, index trackers sell an average of 8.45% of shares outstanding upon deletion, but overall institutional ownership falls by only 1.42%.
We also examine the net trading behavior of active and passive mutual funds and ETFs around S&P 500 index additions and deletions. We identify passive funds in the S12 data using the methodology in Appel, Gromley, and Keim (2016), and we define active funds as all remaining funds. The “Passive” column of TableVIIshows that, on average, passive funds buy a smaller percentage of additions’ shares outstanding than do S&P 500 trackers. This finding is consistent with some additions being migrations, where MidCap-tracking passive funds sell shares to S&P 500-tracking funds. The “Active” column shows that active mutual funds are not the group providing liquidity, as their average net demand is roughly zero. This implies that non-S12 filing institutions (e.g., hedge funds, pension fund, and endowments) are the primary liquidity providers around addition events.
The last hypothesis we consider is that index additions are partially accommodated by firms that issue shares into the rebalance. A few high-profile examples of this, such as CoStar issuing $750 million of new equity concurrent with its addition to the S&P 500 in 2022, suggest this phenomenon may be important. We investigate changes in split-adjusted shares outstanding around all S&P 500 index additions and deletions beginning in 1980. The fraction of firms that issue stock near S&P 500 membership changes increased somewhat in the 2000s, but then declined in the 2010s. Large stock issuances around index changes are rare. Thus, corporate issuance is not a major factor driving the improvement in liquidity around index rebalances.
article.III. Discussion and Extension to Other I:
Our findings suggest the following sequence of events. In the 1980s, index changes were unanticipated, but index funds were small, so addition and deletions effects were relatively modest. As index tracking grew throughout the 1990s, the mispricing deepened and turned into an opportunity. As a result, the market adjusted to take advantage of this opportunity, in part by better anticipating inclusions, and in part by creating arrangements whereby other institutions stood ready to sell to indexers upon inclusion. At the same time, the S&P 500 grew to rely more on index migrations (especially for deletions), which helped reduce overall price impact, and benefitted from increasing assets tracking midcap indices. Together these forces worked to eliminate the index addition and deletion anomalies on average, despite demand shocks that continued to grow in magnitude over the 2000s and 2010s.
Our interpretation raises the question of whether other indices, such as the Russell 2000, or the Nasdaq 100, experience similar reductions in addition and deletion returns. A complete analysis of this question would have to acknowledge the myriad of differences between these indices, including how index changes are announced, how predictable they are, and how capital has tracked these indices over time. Still, it is useful to understand whether the broad patterns we describe for the S&P 500—and as originally highlighted by Bennett, Stulz, and Wang (2020)—exist in other settings.
Below we expand our event return analysis to several other families of indices, choosing to focus on indices for which we can track changes for at least 20 years. To preview our results, we show that, like our findings for the S&P 500, such effects grew from the 1990s to the 2000s and then decreased thereafter for the Russell 1000 and 2000, as well as for the S&P MidCap, S&P SmallCap, and Nasdaq 100. We would like to emphasize, however, that there are differences in magnitudes across the different indices and few of these differences are individually statistically significant.
Each May, Russell ranks firms according to their float-adjusted market capitalization ahead of their annual index reconstitution, which typically occurs on the fourth Friday of June. Before 2007, the 1,000 largest firms would comprise the Russell 1000, while the next 2,000 largest firms would make up the Russell 2000. After 2007, Russell switched to a banding rule, where a firm needed to be more than 2.5% of the total Russell 3000E's capitalization away from the 1000thranked stock to switch indices (see, e.g., Coles, Heath, and Ringgenberg (2022) for more details).
For the Russell 1000 and 2000, there are six types of index changes to consider: (i) firms that switch from the Russell 1000 to the Russell 2000, (ii) firms that switch from the Russell 2000 to the Russell 1000, (iii) additions to the Russell 2000 from outside the Russell 3000 universe, and (iv) additions to the Russell 1000 from outside the Russell 3000 universe, (v) deletions from the Russell 2000 to outside the Russell 3000 universe, and (vi) deletions from the Russell 1000 to outside the Russell 3000 universe. We focus on additions to the Russell 1000 and 2000 from outside the Russell 3000 universe, as these are most comparable to direct additions to the S&P 500. We do not consider deletions from the Russell 3000 to outside the Russell 3000 universe, as these often comprise firms that are delisting and therefore do not pass our sample selection criteria.
We obtain data on Russell index membership from 1990 to 2020 directly from FTSE Russell. To quantify index addition effects, we apply the same methodology we used for S&P 500 index changes with the following minor change: rather than use the cumulative abnormal return from the day before the announcement date to the day after the effective date, we instead use the cumulative market-adjusted return from the day before the ranking date in May to the day after implementation, typically in June (Madhavan (2003)). The market-adjusted return is defined as the return on the stock minus the return on the CRSP value-weighted index over the same period. As with the S&P 500, we exclude firms that were listed, acquired, or delisted for reasons other than an acquisition, as well as firms that were acquirers (i.e., had an ACPERM in CRSP) within 100 days of the index change.
TableVIIIshows that, consistent with Madhavan (2003) and Petajisto (2011), direct additions to the Russell 1000 and 2000 indices were associated with positive returns in the 1990s and with large returns in the 2000s.17Mirroring our results for the S&P 500, these effects grew from the 1990s to the 2000s. This increase may have been driven by an increase in the demand shock associated with index addition, as IWB and IWM, the largest Russell 1000 and 2000 ETFs, were launched in May 2000. In terms of magnitudes, for stocks added to the Russell 2000, addition cumulative market-adjusted returns increase from an average of 2.2% in the 1990s to 8.3% in the 2000s before declining to 3.1% in the 2010s. For stocks added to the Russell 1000, the effect increased from 1% in the 1990s to 17.0% in the 2000s before decreasing to 8.53% in the 2010s. As the bottom rows of the table show, however, none of these individual declines between the 2000s and 2010s are statistically significant.
Unlike changes to the S&P 500, changes to the Russell indices are based on mechanical rules and may therefore be easier to predict. This makes the similarity between the time-series trends for the Russell 1000 and 2000 indices and S&P 500 striking, as changes in predictability are not likely the determinants of a weaker Russell index inclusion effect in recent years. Other factors, such as increased event-specific liquidity arising from prearranged trades, may be more important in explaining the smaller returns to Russell index additions in the 2010s relative to the 2000s.
The broader S&P 1500 universe includes all firms in the S&P 500, the S&P MidCap 400, and the S&P SmallCap 600. These indices are not as widely tracked as the S&P 500, but still attract considerable attention and are traded by mutual funds and ETFs. The same rules of profitability and liquidity for the S&P 500 also apply to their other indices, with the main difference being the size threshold for index inclusion. For the MidCap, there are six types of index changes: (i) additions from outside the S&P universe, (ii) deletions to outside the S&P universe, (iii) additions from the S&P 500, (iv) deletions to the S&P 500, (v) additions from the SmallCap, and (vi) deletions to the SmallCap. For the SmallCap, there are also six types of index changes: (i) additions from outside the S&P universe (ii) deletions to outside the S&P universe (iii) additions from the S&P 500, (iv) deletions to the S&P 500, (v) additions from the MidCap, and (vi) deletions to the MidCap. As with the Russell family of indices, we focus on direct additions to the MidCap and SmallCap from outside the S&P 1500, as well as direct deletions to outside the S&P 1500 universe.
We obtain data on MidCap changes between 1995 and 2020 and SmallCap changes from 1997 to 2020 from Siblis Research. We apply an almost identical methodology for computing index inclusion and deletion effects, with one minor change: Siblis does not provide announcement dates for the MidCap and SmallCap, so we use a window from 10 days before to one day after the index change, as changes are typically announced five business days in advance. As with the S&P 500, we drop firms that were listed, acquired, or delisted for reasons other than an acquisition and firms that were acquirers (i.e., had an ACPERM in CRSP) within 100 days of the index change. Finally, for consistency across benchmarks, as with the Russell index changes, we use cumulative market-adjusted returns.
TableVIIIshows that addition effects for the MidCap increased from the 1990s to the 2000s and decreased thereafter, going from 5.6% in the 1990s, to 8.3% in the 2000s and 5.7% in the 2010s. Similarly, deletion effects grew and then declined in magnitude from –4.5% in the 1990s to –18.3% in the 2000s and –1.2% in the 2010s. The effect of being added to the SmallCap has not changed much over the past 30 years, consistently hovering around 6%, but the effect of being deleted from the SmallCap increased and then decreased, from –6.7% in the 1990s to –24.6% in the 2000s and –12.2% in the 2010s. As with the changes to the Russell indices, except for the increase in deletion returns for the S&P SmallCap index, none of the changes between the 2000s and 2010s are statistically significant.
The Nasdaq 100 comprises the 100 largest nonfinancial companies with a primary listing on the Nasdaq exchange, with a few exceptions as stipulated by index rules. We obtain data on Nasdaq 100 index changes between 1995 and 2020 from Siblis Research. As with the S&P 500, we drop firms that were listed, acquired, or delisted for reasons other than an acquisition and firms that were acquirers (i.e., had an ACPERM in CRSP) within 100 days of the index change. As with the MidCap and SmallCap indices, we do not have announcement dates for the Nasdaq 100, but these changes are typically announced five business days in advance so we use a window of 10 days before the change to one day after when computing index addition and deletion effects. Finally, as we describe above, we focus on market-adjusted returns for our sample of Nasdaq index changes.
TableVIIIshows a steady decline in the effect of being added to the Nasdaq 100, going from 3.9% in the 1990s to 2.6% in the 2000s and 2.0% in the 2010s. The deletion effects are consistently minimal, hovering near zero over the past 30 years. Once again, none of the declines from the 2000s to the 2010s is statistically significant.
Overall, the results for the Russell 1000 and 2000, S&P MidCap and SmallCap, and Nasdaq 100 paint a picture consistent with our main results: index addition and deletion effects grew into the 2000s and shrunk thereafter, but the statistical significance for these other indices is weak. In column (9) of TableVIII, we pool together all of the additions from the Russell 1000, Russell 2000, S&P MidCap, S&P SmallCap, and Nasdaq 100. The results mirror the patterns documented in the individual indices, showing a decline of 4.2% in addition effects across all indices, significant at the 5% level. Similarly, in column (10) of TableVIII, we pool together all of the deletions from the S&P MidCap, S&P SmallCap, and Nasdaq 100. Here, the deletion effect shrinks by an average of 10.3 percentage points between the 2000s and 2010s, statistically significant at the 1% level.
article.IV. Conclusion:
According to efficient markets theory, if a class of investors were to buy or sell a stock for reasons unrelated to fundamentals, well-capitalized arbitrageurs should respond aggressively to provide liquidity, limiting the price impact. The well-known index effect, whereby a stock added to an index such as the S&P 500 goes up in price, is often held up as an example of market inefficiency. The notion of a downward-sloping demand curve is a key ingredient in most behavioral finance models of the stock market.
Over the past decade, the well-known index effect for the S&P 500 has disappeared, with the average addition or deletion experiencing abnormal returns near zero. In this paper, we consider four explanations for why this may have happened. To summarize, our assessment is that the decline in the index effect is driven primarily by two factors: the role of migrations from the S&P MidCap Index, as well as an overall increase in the market's ability to provide liquidity to index changes. A third factor—increased predictability of index changes—also plays a small role. Our overall conclusion is that, when demand shocks become regular and repeated, competitive markets adapt over time to minimize price impact, in the spirit of Lo's (2004) adaptive markets hypothesis.
While our findings uncover and quantify the forces driving the decline of the index effect, they leave open another puzzle: while S&P 500 index changes have been around since 1957, it took until the 2010s for the market to evolve to neutralize the price impact. Why did it take so long? And why did it take much longer for the market to adapt than in the context of other anomalies related to informational efficiency? We expect subsequent work to explore these questions.
Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon

AItitle: 标普500指数效应消退：流动性弹性跃升颠覆需求冲击定价
AIsummary: **论文总结**  该研究聚焦金融市场的"指数效应"（指数成分股调整引发的异常收益），旨在解释为何标普500指数成分股调整的异常收益从1990年代的7.4%降至近十年的不足1%。传统理论认为，指数调整会因被动资金流动产生价格压力（Shleifer, 1986），但这一现象近年显著弱化。作者通过多维度分析发现，原因包括：  1. **迁移效应**：约70%的调整涉及标普中型股与大盘股的迁移，双向资金流动抵消了净需求冲击；  2. **流动性提升**：做市商与主动投资者通过算法和机构协作，大幅降低交易摩擦（流动性弹性提升20倍）；  3. **成分股特征变化**（仅次要作用），以及部分调整可预测性增加。  研究通过事件分析、回归模型（控制公司规模、套利风险等变量）及跨指数验证（罗素、纳斯达克100等）证实结论。其核心贡献在于揭示市场通过"适应性进化"（如流动性机制创新）逐步消化非基本面需求冲击，呼应了Lo（2004）的适应性市场假说，为理解市场效率的动态演变提供新视角。（248字）
Highlights:
学术要点 1：迁移效应（Migration Effect） 指成分股在不同市值指数间迁移时，跟踪不同指数的基金形成对冲性买卖压力，显著降低净需求冲击的现象。|||  
学术要点 2：套利风险（Arbitrage Risk） 基于Wurgler-Zhuravskaya模型衡量的股票可替代性程度，反映做市商对冲指数变更冲击的难易程度及其对价格压力的影响。|||  
学术要点 3：前驱交易（Front-Running） 市场参与者通过预测指数变更提前建仓，导致价格效应在公告日前已被部分消化的策略性交易行为。|||  
学术要点 4：事件特定流动性（Event-Specific Liquidity） 针对指数调整事件形成的专项流动性供给机制，包括投行专用交易团队和算法交易系统的定向部署。|||  
学术要点 5：适应性市场假说（Adaptive Markets Hypothesis） 解释市场通过制度创新（如指数迁移机制）和参与者行为调整（如机构反向提供流动性），动态消除持续性异常回报的现象。
